notes: Table OCR showed $62; normalized to S62

name: Sockeye salmon
notes: Weight from Tucker 1970 text (5°C); speed and metabolic rate need Brett 1963 Trans R Soc Can Vol1(4):441-457 to extract

name: European eel
notes: Silver eel; 65 cm/s is high end of tested range where CoT is lowest (0.329); metabolic_rate inferred as CoT × speed

name: European eel
notes: Green eel; 35 cm/s low end of tested range; CoT 0.417; metabolic_rate inferred; anguilliform vs carangiform propulsion — similar CoT

name: Pacific bluefin tuna (juvenile)
notes: Minimum O2 = 222 mg O2/kg/hr at 0.75-1.0 BL/s; body length ~47 cm assumed; endothermic — high basal rate elevates metabolic CoT despite superb hydrodynamics

name: Yellowfin tuna (juvenile)
notes: Minimum O2 = 164 mg O2/kg/hr at 0.65 BL/s; body length ~42 cm assumed; endothermic; metabolic CoT comparable to or higher than ectothermic fish of similar size

name: Human on bicycle (touring)
notes: Touring cyclist: 83 kg with machine; 66 kg rider; 5.5 sq ft frontal area; 50 psi tyres; 0.5 hp; total metabolic 24 kcal/min; weight_kg=70+17 kg (reference person + Whitt touring machine mass 83-66); met and CoT rescaled from prior per-70-kg row

name: Human on bicycle (touring)
notes: Touring cyclist; 0.4 hp; total metabolic 19.5 kcal/min; weight_kg=70+17 kg; met and CoT rescaled from prior per-70-kg row

name: Human on bicycle (touring)
notes: Touring cyclist; 0.3 hp; total metabolic 15 kcal/min; weight_kg=70+17 kg; met and CoT rescaled from prior per-70-kg row

name: Human on bicycle (touring)
notes: Touring cyclist; 0.2 hp; total metabolic 10.5 kcal/min; weight_kg=70+17 kg; met and CoT rescaled from prior per-70-kg row

notes: Touring cyclist; 0.1 hp; total metabolic 6 kcal/min; weight_kg=70+17 kg; met and CoT rescaled from prior per-70-kg row

name: Human on bicycle (touring)
notes: Touring cyclist; 0.05 hp; total metabolic 3.75 kcal/min; near minimum CoT; weight_kg=70+17 kg; met and CoT rescaled from prior per-70-kg row

name: Human on bicycle (touring)
notes: Touring cyclist; 0.025 hp; total metabolic 2.65 kcal/min; minimum total CoT in table; weight_kg=70+17 kg (reference person + Whitt touring machine); met and CoT rescaled from 83 kg system row

name: Human on bicycle (touring)
notes: Touring cyclist; 0.008 hp; total metabolic 1.9 kcal/min; CoT rises at very low speed; weight_kg=70+17 kg; met and CoT rescaled from prior per-70-kg row

name: Human on bicycle (racing)
notes: Racing position; ~3.65 sq ft frontal area; Wilson's estimate from Whitt 1971; the iconic point added to Tucker's figure in Scientific American; weight_kg=70+7 kg (reference person + ~7 kg racing bicycle); met and CoT rescaled from rider-only 70 kg row; exemplar=0 (duplicate modality vs touring bicycle—single Bicycle point on figures uses touring exemplar)

name: Velomobile
notes: weight_kg=70+15 kg (reference person + velomobile shell); CdA=0.12 m² (typical recumbent fairing); Crr=0.004; 23.6% gross efficiency (Whitt); at minimum-CoT speed ~25 km/h; typical modern velomobile e.g. WAW or Alleweder; met and CoT per kg rescaled from same total power as prior 70 kg row
source: Engineering model (Whitt 1971 efficiency; velomobile aerodynamics literature)

name: Velomobile
notes: At 30 km/h; CdA=0.12 m²; Crr=0.004; aero drag rising; CoT slightly above minimum; at higher speeds velomobile advantage over bicycle grows dramatically; weight_kg=70+15 kg; met and CoT rescaled from prior 70 kg row
source: Engineering model (Whitt 1971 efficiency; velomobile aerodynamics literature)

name: Cross-country skiing (skating)
notes: Modern skating-technique ski (2004 model); net CoT=1.917 J/kg/m (Table 1); at 'migration' speed (7 W/kg net; 7-8 h sustainable); gross adds standing rest 1.163 W/kg; Vuokatti climate-controlled ski tunnel; non-elite subjects; weight_kg=70+3 kg (reference person + skis/boots/poles; matches ~73 kg subjects)
source: Formenti et al. 2005 Proc R Soc B 272:1561

name: Cross-country skiing (skating)
notes: Skating technique; at 'hunting' speed (9 W/kg net; 3-4 h sustainable); net CoT speed-independent (like running); gross CoT falls slightly with speed; weight_kg=70+3 kg
source: Formenti et al. 2005 Proc R Soc B 272:1561

name: Cross-country skiing (classical)
notes: Modern diagonal-stride ski (2004 DS model); net CoT=2.106 J/kg/m (Table 1); at 'migration' speed; classical technique ~10% more expensive than skating technique; weight_kg=70+3 kg
source: Formenti et al. 2005 Proc R Soc B 272:1561

name: Cross-country skiing (classical)
notes: Classical diagonal-stride; at 'hunting' speed (9 W/kg net); both XC techniques ~25-30% cheaper per km than running; modern skiing cost ≈ optimum walking (paper conclusion); weight_kg=70+3 kg
source: Formenti et al. 2005 Proc R Soc B 272:1561

name: Inline skating
notes: Recreational inline skating at 14.5 km/h; VO2=26.7 ml/kg/min (×0.303=8.09 kcal/kg/hr); near minimum-CoT speed; flat asphalt; no drafting; comparable to walking efficiency; weight_kg=70+2 kg (skates/gear); met and CoT rescaled from 70 kg row
source: Wallick et al. 1995 Med Sci Sports Exerc 27:242

name: Inline skating
notes: Inline skating at 21.7 km/h; VO2=42.0 ml/kg/min; CoT rises above minimum at this speed; always within ~15% of walking CoT across typical skating speeds; weight_kg=70+2 kg; met and CoT rescaled from 70 kg row

name: Human (front crawl)
notes: Front crawl at 1.0 m/s (3.6 km/h); gross energy cost ≈600 J/m for ~70 kg swimmer; near minimum CoT for front crawl; ~3× more expensive than walking; humans are poor swimmers
source: Pendergast et al. 1978 J Appl Physiol 44:475

name: Human (front crawl)
notes: Front crawl at 1.2 m/s (competitive training pace); gross energy ≈700 J/m; CoT rises with speed due to drag; ~25× more expensive per km than bicycle; human propulsive efficiency ~25% vs fish ~80%
source: Pendergast et al. 1978 J Appl Physiol 44:475

name: Ice speed skating
notes: Speed skating at 20 km/h; VO2 est. ~25 ml/kg/min; ice μ≈0.003 (10× lower than road tyre); near minimum CoT speed; significantly better than inline skating (0.56) due to far lower friction; weight_kg=70+2 kg (boots/blades); met and CoT rescaled from 70 kg row
source: di Prampero et al. 1976 J Appl Physiol 40:584

name: Ice speed skating
notes: At 30 km/h; VO2 est. ~35 ml/kg/min; CoT near minimum for ice skating; combines low friction with efficient gliding stride mechanics; weight_kg=70+2 kg; met and CoT rescaled from 70 kg row
source: di Prampero et al. 1976 J Appl Physiol 40:584

name: Rowing (single scull)
notes: Single scull at 12 km/h; VO2 est. ~28 ml/kg/min; moderate training pace; wave drag + body oscillation overhead makes rowing expensive per km; comparable to walking; weight_kg=70+5 kg (reference person + shell/oars)

name: Rowing (single scull)
notes: At 15 km/h; VO2 est. ~38 ml/kg/min; CoT rises with speed; surface wave drag ∝v³; despite looking fast and fluid CoT similar to walking per km; weight_kg=70+5 kg

name: Kayak (K1)
notes: K1 kayak at slow touring pace 8 km/h; VO2 est. ~22 ml/kg/min; arm-muscle-dominated propulsion (gross efficiency ~12-15% vs ~23% for legs); wave drag even at low speed; weight_kg=70+5 kg (reference person + K1 hull/paddle)
source: Tesch et al. 1976 Med Sci Sports Exerc

name: Kayak (K1)
notes: At ~1000m race pace (3.25 m/s = 11.7 km/h); VO2 ~53 ml/kg/min (Tesch 1983: 4.67 L/min at race pace); near VO2max; wave drag + arm inefficiency makes kayak costly per km at racing speed; weight_kg=70+5 kg
source: Tesch et al. 1976 / Tesch 1983 Med Sci Sports Exerc

name: Boeing 787-9 Dreamliner
notes: MTOW 254,700 kg; economic cruise 880 km/h (Mach 0.83) at 11,000 m; fuel flow 5,614 kg/hr (verified); Jet-A LHV 10,320 kcal/kg; GEnx-1B70/P2 × 2; 2.2× more fuel-efficient per kg than DC-8 era

name: Airbus A350-900
notes: MTOW 280,000 kg; cruise 903 km/h (Mach 0.85); fuel flow est. ~5,500 kg/hr; Trent XWB-84 × 2; ~10% more efficient per unit mass than 787-9; most fuel-efficient widebody in production 2020s
source: Airbus A350-900 specification / ICAO fuel data

name: Paraglider (EN-C class)
notes: Total mass 90 kg (75 kg pilot + 15 kg harness + wing); L/D≈10.5:1 at best-glide 38 km/h; CoT from gravitational PE loss (sink 1.005 m/s → 8.48 kcal/kg/hr) + pilot resting (0.89); thermalling gives effectively zero net CoT

name: Hang glider (rigid wing)
notes: Total mass 90 kg; modern rigid-wing L/D≈15:1 at best-glide 55 km/h; sink 1.019 m/s → gravity power 8.60 kcal/kg/hr + pilot resting 0.89; approaches racing bicycle efficiency

name: Daedalus 88 (human-powered)
notes: Record flight 104 kg (32 kg aircraft + 72 kg pilot Kanellopoulos); for cross-mode comparison weight_kg=70+32 kg (reference person + same airframe mass); total metabolic power rescaled from 104 kg row; 115 km Crete→Santorini in 3h55m; pilot output ~215 W; still less efficient than cycling due to lift cost

name: Bottlenose dolphin
notes: Minimum CoT = 1.29 J/kg/m at 2.1 m/s (direct respirometry on trained dolphins); endothermic yet efficient due to streamlined fusiform body and oscillatory caudal propulsion; wave-riding can halve this cost; 8-12× more efficient than human swimmers
source: Williams et al. 1993 J Exp Biol 179:31

name: Killer whale (orca)
notes: Optimal speed 2.6-3.0 m/s (field respiration rates in N. resident killer whales); min CoT approaches allometric prediction COT_TOT = 7.79 × m^-0.29 (Williams 1999); observed average speed 1.6 m/s lower than optimal — whales don't always minimise cost
source: Williams & Noren 2009 Marine Mammal Sci; Williams 1999 Phil Trans R Soc B 354:193

name: Humpback whale
notes: Allometric estimate: COT_TOT = 7.79 × 35000^-0.29 = 0.375 J/kg/m; cruise ~2 m/s; large body size reduces mass-specific CoT; swimming specialists (dolphins to large whales) follow same allometric scaling as running mammals
source: Williams 1999 Phil Trans R Soc B 354:193

name: Blue whale
notes: Largest animal on Earth (~130,000 kg); allometric: COT_TOT = 7.79 × 130000^-0.29 = 0.256 J/kg/m; cruise ~2 m/s; mass-specific CoT approaches ectothermic fish despite endothermy — size scaling advantage overwhelms thermoregulation cost
source: Williams 1999 Phil Trans R Soc B 354:193

name: West Indian manatee
notes: COT_TOT = 0.87 ± 0.17 J/kg/m (directly measured via flow-through respirometry during submerged swimming); resting metabolic rate only 0.36× predicted for placental mammals (Kleiber); sirenians evolved in warm water and sacrificed high metabolic rate for low energy budget
source: John et al. 2021 J Exp Biol 224:jeb237628

name: Green sea turtle
notes: Resting VO2 ≈ 0.024 L/kg/hr; swimming VO2 ≈ 0.068 L/kg/hr at 0.55 m/s (2.83× resting; 5.05 kcal/L O2); ectotherm → very low basal metabolic rate; highly streamlined carapace; among the most efficient non-fish swimmers measured

name: Squid (market squid)
notes: CoT = 12.5 J/kg/m at 0.36 m/s; jet propulsion Froude efficiency ~30-40% vs fish undulatory ~80-90%; active VO2 = 862 mL O2/kg/hr; ~5× more expensive than salmon of the same size; burst escape jets are anaerobic and even more costly; pays with speed and maneuverability

name: Adélie penguin
notes: Minimum CoT = 7.9 J/kg/m at optimal speed 2.0 m/s (swim flume); metabolic rate 15.8 W/kg at cruise; underwater wing-propulsion (oscillating flippers); far cheaper per km than any walking penguin; endothermic with Antarctic maintenance costs
source: Culik et al. 1994 J Comp Physiol B 164:597

name: Emperor penguin (swimming)
notes: Estimated from allometric scaling of penguin swimming CoT (∝ m^-0.29 from Adélie data); speed 2.3 m/s per Sato et al. 2010 (all penguins cruise 1.8-2.3 m/s to minimize CoT); emperors are the most efficient penguin swimmers by body mass
source: Sato et al. 2010 Proc R Soc B; Ponganis 1991 J Exp Biol

name: Emperor penguin (waddling)
notes: CoT = 8.64 J/kg/m at preferred walking speed 0.5 m/s; ~2× more expensive than other animals of similar body mass due to upright posture and short legs forcing rapid force generation; recovers 80% mechanical energy per stride (Cavagna 2002 Nature) but muscles still work hard; waddling costs MORE per km than swimming
source: Le Maho et al. 1980 J Appl Physiol 49:888

name: Harbor seal (adult)
notes: Min CoT = 2.3 J/kg/m measured via indirect calorimetry in flow channel at 1.0–1.4 m/s; hind-flipper undulation propulsion; semiaquatic mammal yet approaches fully aquatic marine mammal allometric (Williams 1999: 2.14 J/kg/m for 70 kg); ~2× more expensive than bottlenose dolphin; blubber streamlines body; skill and body composition improve dramatically from yearling to adult

name: Harbor seal (yearling)
notes: Min CoT = 3.6 J/kg/m at same flow channel speed; 57% more expensive per km than adult seal; still developing swimming musculature and streamlining; mass-specific CoT for semiaquatic mammals is 2.5–5× higher than fully aquatic cetaceans (Williams 1999); skill acquisition and body form are major drivers of efficiency

name: Nurse shark
notes: Min CoT = 68 mg O2/kg/km at 23°C at 0.33 BL/s (from polynomial fit to speed trials); lowest SMR (36 mg O2/kg/hr) and RMR (95 mg O2/kg/hr) of any shark species measured — benthic ambush lifestyle; ectothermic; near-anguilliform swimming kinematics; efficient per km because resting metabolism is so low that cruise barely increments it
source: Dowd et al. 2015 J Exp Mar Biol Ecol 465:26

name: Mako shark
notes: RMR 344 mg O2/kg/hr at routine swim speed 0.44 BL/s in respirometer; highest SMR (124 mg O2/kg/hr) of any shark — regional endothermy (red muscle, viscera, cranium maintained above ambient); elevated resting cost is the price of endothermy; thunniform swimmer with high propulsive efficiency; in wild cruises 2–3 BL/s where CoT would improve; stark contrast with nurse shark despite shared body plan

name: Discoid cockroach
notes: VO2ss = 0.52 + 3.1V (mL O2/g/hr); at maximum tested speed 0.48 km/h; net CoT slope = 3.1 mL O2/g/km = 15.5 kcal/kg/km; 2–3× above mammal allometric for this mass; uses same rapid linear aerobic response as vertebrates (t½ on-response <90 s); tracheal O2 delivery without circulatory system gives cockroaches a surprisingly mammal-like exercise physiology

name: American cockroach
notes: Min net CoT = 9.83 mL O2/g/km = 49.2 kcal/kg/km; gross CoT at ~1.5 km/h approaches this asymptote; fastest cockroach runner tested; ~5× above mammal allometric line for its mass; despite the high mass-specific metabolic rate of small ectotherms, the cockroach falls in recognisable distance of the allometric prediction; can reach 1.5 m/s in bursts but max sustained aerobic speed ~0.4 m/s

name: VLCC crude oil tanker
notes: Modern 300,000 DWT VLCC (e.g. TI-class or Suezmax derivative); loaded displacement 345,000 tonnes; service speed 14.5 knots at Beaufort 3; two-stroke slow-speed diesel ~28 MW; HFO 115 t/day = 4,792 kg/hr; HFO LHV 10,200 kcal/kg; by far the most efficient transport mode by mass (≈30× better than bicycle per km per kg); efficiency arises from: (1) displacement hull generates no lift-induced drag, (2) Froude number ~0.06 so wave-making drag tiny at low speed, (3) enormous mass amortises engine overhead, (4) large-diameter slow-turning propeller gives ~70% Froude efficiency; shipping moves ~90% of world trade
source: gCaptain maritime forum / IMO Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI) framework

name: Container ship (Triple-E class)
notes: Maersk Triple-E class at 19-knot design cruise (35.2 km/h); total loaded displacement 251,000 t (55,000 t light + 196,000 DWT); twin MAN 8S80ME-C9.2 engines 29.68 MW each; SFC=168 g/kWh; power at 19 kn ≈ 26,000 kW (cubed speed scaling from 25-kn max); fuel 4,368 kg/hr HFO; design slow-steaming strategy (vs 25-kn predecessors) lowers CO2/TEU by 50%; 18,270 TEU capacity; most fuel-efficient large container ship when ordered (2013); CoT similar to VLCC despite higher speed because enormous mass overwhelms fuel-burn rate
source: Wikipedia: Triple E-class container ship; MAN engine data

name: Diesel freight train (heavy haul)
notes: 120-car loaded coal train; 15,600 t cargo + 4× EMD SD70 locos (720 t) = 16,320 t total; cruise 70 km/h; rolling resistance Crr=0.0015 (steel-on-steel, ~5× lower than car tyres); rolling drag 240,163 N + aero drag 2,774 N = 4,723 kW propulsion; diesel BSFC 185 g/kWh → 874 kg/hr fuel; diesel LHV 8,800 kcal/kg → 7,689 kcal/hr total; consistent with CSX fleet average 520 RTM/gal (system includes empties, stops, grades); freight train CoT closely matches VLCC ship (0.0053) — both exploit huge mass and low contact resistance; steel-on-steel rolling resistance is ~5× lower than rubber-on-road
source: AAR (Association of American Railroads) freight data; EMD SD70 BSFC specification

name: Suburban electric train (S-Bahn)
notes: DB Class 423 S-Bahn Munich; 4-car EMU; 138 tonnes; cruise 100 km/h; rolling power 75 kW (Crr=0.002 × 138 t) + aero 46 kW (CdA≈3.5 m²) + 130 kW auxiliary (HVAC, traction electronics) = 251 kW ≈ 2.5 kWh/km; installed 1,200 kW (4×300 kW); regenerative braking returns ~25% of braking energy; electric traction efficiency 88%; more efficient than TGV per kg-km because aero drag ∝ v³ — at 100 km/h drag is 27× smaller than at 300 km/h; CoT between freight (0.007) and TGV (0.045)
source: DB Bahn Class 423 technical data; aerodynamic physics model

name: TGV Réseau (high-speed train)
reference: Fritz et al. 2018 / ATOC Greengauge report
notes: TGV Réseau: 2 power cars + 8 trailers; 383 tonnes; 8,800 kW installed; service speed 300 km/h; from Fritz et al. 2018 energy study: ICE 3 at 330 km/h = 59 Wh/seat/km; TGV at 300 km/h ≈ 50 Wh/seat/km (slightly less speed and better L/D); 404 seats × 50 = 20,200 Wh/km = 6,060 kW total draw; rolling power 626 kW (Crr=0.002) + aero 2,487 kW (CdA≈7 m² at 83.3 m/s) + 950 kW auxiliary = 4,063 kW propulsion + overhead; aerodynamic drag rises as v³ — TGV pays 3× the aero penalty of S-Bahn per kg despite lower total power; CoT rises with speed (same physics as all vehicles)
source: Fritz et al. 2018 Transp. Syst. Technol. 4(3 suppl.1):134-155; ATOC/Greengauge 21 report 2009

name: Transrapid TR08 maglev
notes: Transrapid TR08, 5-section vehicle; mass ~282 tonnes; Hamburg–Berlin design; from Fritz et al. 2018 (Table comparison Fig. 4): 63 Wh/seat/km at 430 km/h — lowest of any system at this speed, 7% above ICE 3 at 330 km/h (59 Wh) despite 30% higher speed; 310 seats × 63 = 19,530 Wh/km; power 19.5×430 = 8,385 kW; no rolling resistance (levitation) but magnetic drag + eddy current resistance; at 450 km/h Transrapid = 71–76 Wh/Pl/km; slightly worse CoT than TGV (0.045) due to higher speed aerodynamic losses — air resistance dominates above 400 km/h; maglev advantage is zero wheel wear, not energy (below 400 km/h its energy profile is nearly identical to wheel-rail HSR)
source: Fritz et al. 2018 Transp. Syst. Technol. 4(3 suppl.1):134-155 doi 10.17816/transsyst201843s1134-155

name: Urban tram (electric)
reference: Alstom Citadis specification / UIC tram data
notes: Modern 3-section low-floor tram (e.g. Alstom Citadis X05 Paris); 41.6 tonnes; city service average 25 km/h (including stops); ~3 kWh/km in service (typical European urban tram from UIC data); 3×860=2,580 kcal/km; tram is LESS efficient per kg-km than TGV or S-Bahn because: (1) light mass means low denominator in CoT despite low energy use, (2) frequent stops mean kinetic energy repeatedly wasted (regenerative braking recovers ~25% only), (3) slow speed — CoT = energy/km ÷ mass, and for trams energy/km is not proportionally lower than heavier trains; trams still 2× more efficient than a human walking per kg-km
source: Alstom Citadis X05 specification; UIC tram energy benchmarking

name: F1D indoor rubber free-flight
reference: FAI Free Flight rules / BMFA rubber monograph
notes: Ultra-optimized indoor duration model; FAI class F1D: min airframe 1.2g + max 0.4g rubber motor = 1.6g total; rubber energy density 5.5 J/g (BMFA monograph: 3725 ft-lb/lb for modern FAI rubber); total stored energy 2.2 J; speed 0.7 m/s (2–3 ft/s, FAI F1D blog); L/D ≈ 17; large 550mm-diameter slow-turning propeller at ~50 RPM gives ~72% Froude efficiency; 30+ min flights in large hangars; power = 2.2 J / 1800 s = 0.00122 W; per kg = 0.763 W/kg; CoT matches modern wide-body airliner per kg — extraordinary for 1.6g aircraft; propeller size-to-model-mass ratio exceeds any other powered aircraft
source: FAI Sporting Code Vol F1 (Free Flight); BMFA Rubber Monograph; J&H Aerospace F1D data

name: Pennyplane (indoor rubber)
notes: Standard AMA/FAI Pennyplane: 3.2g balsa airframe + ~1.3g 3/32-inch rubber motor = 4.5g total (cross-section 2.38×1mm; loop ~61cm; density 1.1 g/cm³); energy 1.3×5.5=7.15J; forward speed ~1.0 m/s; 13.35 min best time in large hangar (J&H Aerospace data); total distance ~801m; much heavier and less optimized than F1D but still CoT comparable to cross-country skis; 12-inch propeller; entry-level indoor endurance model; demonstrates that even simple rubber free-flight models are highly aerodynamically efficient
source: J&H Aerospace Pennyplane specification; BMFA Rubber Monograph (5.5 J/g rubber)

name: Quadcopter drone (consumer)
notes: DJI Mini 3 Pro; total mass 249g; battery 7.38V × 2453 mAh = 18.1 Wh; max range 18 km at 43.2 km/h (DJI spec — this is the range-optimal speed where energy/distance is minimized); power at cruise = 18.1 Wh / (18 km / 43.2 km/h) = 43.4 W; CoT = 3.47 kcal/kg/km; multirotor is 10× less efficient than fixed-wing of similar mass because all lift is from rotor thrust (no wing); CoT similar to a hummingbird in forward flight — both are biological/mechanical rotorcraft — and ~13× worse than a racing bicycle; quadcopter CoT improves only slightly with speed as parasite drag rises to partially offset improving induced power

name: Electric RC model airplane
notes: Typical 1m-wingspan electric park flyer (e.g. E-flite Apprentice S 15e class); mass ~1.1 kg AUW; 3S LiPo 2200mAh 11.1V = 24.4 Wh; 22 min typical flight; average cruise power ~65 W (full-throttle 300 W for climb; ~20% throttle cruise); electric motor+ESC efficiency ~75% combined; distance per charge: 70 km/h × 0.367 hr = 25.7 km; CoT = (24.4 Wh × 3.6 kJ/Wh / 4.186 kJ/kcal) / (1.1 kg × 25.7 km) = 0.73 kcal/kg/km; nearly identical CoT to a pigeon or starling; electric drive is far more efficient than small IC (glow/petrol) engines which have ~10% thermal efficiency at model scales giving CoT ~40 kcal/kg/km; wings = efficient, motor = efficient
source: Generic electric park flyer specifications; aerodynamic first principles

name: Wandering albatross
reference: Adams et al. 1986; Weimerskirch et al. 2000
notes: Breeding adult, ~8.7 kg; doubly-labelled water study (Adams et al. 1986 Physiol. Zool. 59:583): flight metabolic rate = 2.35× measured BMR; BMR for 8.7 kg non-passerine = 78.3×8.7^0.723 = 374 kcal/day = 15.6 kcal/hr total = 1.79 kcal/kg/hr; flight rate = 2.35×1.79 = 4.21 kcal/kg/hr; average GPS ground speed 54 km/h from Weimerskirch et al. 2000 (Proc Roy Soc B 267:1869); DYNAMIC SOARING: albatross harvests energy from wind shear by alternately diving downwind and soaring upwind — this is why flight costs only 2.35× BMR vs 8-12× for flapping birds of comparable mass; at 0.078 kcal/kg/km the albatross is more efficient per kg-km than a racing bicycle (0.15); in favourable winds ground speed reaches 80 km/h → CoT drops to 0.053 kcal/kg/km; the most energy-efficient powered locomotion in the animal kingdom
source: Adams et al. 1986 Physiol Zool 59:583-591; Weimerskirch et al. 2000 Proc Roy Soc B 267:1869-1874

name: Dark-bellied brent goose
notes: Mass ~1.3 kg; migration cruise ~65 km/h (18 m/s = Vmr for this wing loading); flight metabolic rate estimated at 7.5× BMR following Butler & Woakes (1980 J Exp Biol 85:169, heart rate telemetry in barnacle geese Branta leucopsis, a sister species); BMR for 1.3 kg non-passerine = 78.3×1.3^0.723 = 94.7 kcal/day = 3.95 kcal/hr = 3.04 kcal/kg/hr; 7.5× BMR = 22.8 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 22.8/65 = 0.351 kcal/kg/km; dark-bellied brent breeds in Siberia and winters in NW Europe, staging through the Wadden Sea — one of the great long-distance migrations; small Branta geese are efficient because: (1) high aspect-ratio wings give low induced drag, (2) V-formation reduces power ~15% for trailing birds, (3) large wing area relative to mass reduces wing loading compared to dabbling ducks; marked contrast to the albatross — the goose must flap continuously, paying the full aerodynamic cost
source: Butler & Woakes 1980 J Exp Biol 85:169; Pennycuick 1989 aerodynamic model

name: Common cuckoo
notes: Mass ~110g; migration airspeed ~60 km/h (from satellite-tagged UK cuckoos, BTO / Hewson et al. 2016 Nat Commun 7:12296); flight metabolic rate estimated at 9× BMR (typical for medium-small non-passerine flapping migrants, between geese 7-8× and passerines 10-13×); BMR for 0.11 kg non-passerine = 78.3×0.11^0.723 = 15.87 kcal/day = 0.661 kcal/hr = 6.01 kcal/kg/hr; 9× BMR = 54.1 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 54.1/60 = 0.902 kcal/kg/km; REMARKABLE MIGRATION: UK cuckoos fly ~16,000 km to sub-Saharan Africa, making non-stop Sahara crossings of up to 90 hours; the BTO satellite tracking revealed two routes — a western route via Spain and a shorter eastern route via Italy — with higher survival on the eastern route; cuckoo CoT is comparable to a migrant songbird despite 4× larger mass because their low BMR (non-passerine) keeps the absolute energy cost per hour low, while higher speed spreads that cost over more km/h; value is an estimate — no direct respirometry on flying cuckoos exists
source: Hewson et al. 2016 Nat Commun 7:12296 (BTO satellite tracking); allometric BMR (Lasiewski & Dawson 1967)

name: Thrush nightingale
notes: Mass ~26g (average during 12-hr wind tunnel flight, starting ~30g, ending ~22g); one bird flew 176 hr cumulative at 10 m/s (36 km/h) in the Lund wind tunnel, equivalent to 6,300 km; flight power estimated at 1.91 W from energy balance (mass loss during flight + recovery feeding balance); per kg: 1.91/0.026 = 73.5 W/kg = 63.2 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 63.2/36 = 1.756 kcal/kg/km; the paper notes this is LOW compared to aerodynamic models for birds of similar mass — suggesting migratory birds may have lower flight costs than non-migrants through physiological adaptation (larger pectoral muscles, more oxidative muscle fibres); speed of 10 m/s was chosen as predicted maximum range speed (Vmr); thrush nightingale breeds in NE Europe and winters in SE Africa (16,000 km round trip); this is one of very few direct metabolic measurements during sustained long-distance migratory flight in a wind tunnel
source: Klaassen et al. 2000 Condor 102:444-451 (wind tunnel energy balance)

name: Barn swallow
notes: Mass ~20g (lean bird; migrating birds carry fat load up to 30g); metabolic power from empirical formula e(f) = 0.38 × m^0.58 W (m in grams) measured in free-flying barn swallows via heart rate calibration at Groningen; for 20g: e = 0.38 × 20^0.58 = 2.73 W → per kg = 136.5 W/kg; but correction for metabolic overhead: 117.4 kcal/kg/hr = 136.5 W/kg × (3600 s/hr) / (4186 J/kcal); at cruise speed 40 km/h (11.1 m/s; observed free-flight mean 8-9 m/s, Bäckman & Alerstam 2002); CoT = 117.4/40 = 2.94 kcal/kg/km; barn swallow migrates 10,000 km from Europe to sub-Saharan Africa, fuelling from aerial insect prey en route (fly-and-forage migration); unlike larger migrants that stage at stopover sites, swallows can feed while flying; IMPORTANT NOTE: because swallows are obligate flappers (no dynamic soaring, no energy saving wing design like albatross), they must pay the full aerodynamic cost; their pointed wings give good aerodynamic efficiency but this cannot overcome the mass-specific metabolic cost that rises steeply for small birds
source: Turner AK 2006 / Bäckman & Alerstam 2002 Proc Roy Soc B 269:1011-1017; Park et al. 2001 J Exp Biol 204:2741-2750

name: Fowler's toad (slow walk)
notes: Fowler's toad (formerly Bufo woodhousei fowleri); 25.8g; slow walk at 0.09 km/h; VO2 = 0.13 (resting) + 3.85×0.09 = 0.477 ml O2/g/hr (linear aerobic portion); metabolic rate = 0.477×5 = 2.385 kcal/kg/hr; hopping/walking gait at this slow speed; resting VO2 = 0.13 ml/g/hr; toads WALK at slow speeds and switch to hopping at higher speeds; CoT is HIGHER at slower speeds because resting metabolic overhead is large relative to locomotion; ectotherm with very low resting rate but also low aerobic scope; compares poorly to similarly-sized mice (~3 kcal/kg/km) or lizards — anuran saltatory gait does not provide the elastic energy savings seen in large kangaroos

name: Fowler's toad (max aerobic hop)
notes: At maximum aerobic speed 0.27 km/h; VO2 = 1.17 ml O2/g/hr (9× resting = 9×0.13); this is VO2max for sustained locomotion; metabolic rate = 1.17×5 = 5.85 kcal/kg/hr; minimum gross CoT occurs at maximum aerobic speed (same pattern as all terrestrial animals); at speeds >0.27 km/h VO2 plateaus but speed increases → apparent aerobic CoT falls, but this is misleading: extra power is anaerobic (lactate) and unsustainable; hopping in small anurans is NOT more economical than running (unlike large kangaroos which save elastic energy); CoT ~8× a similarly-sized mouse due to sprawling posture, saltatory inefficiency, and low aerobic scope; ectothermy provides low resting cost but doesn't make exercise cheap

name: Sidewinder rattlesnake
notes: 8 snakes mean mass 110g; sustained sidewinding on treadmill at 0.5 km/h for 33-180 min; net CoT = 0.408 ml O2/g/km (Secor et al. 1992 J Exp Biol 163:1-14); gross CoT = net + resting/speed = (0.408×5) + (0.05×5/0.5) = 2.04 + 0.50 = 2.54 kcal/kg/km; gross metabolic rate = 2.54×0.5 = 1.27 kcal/kg/hr; SIDEWINDING is the most efficient snake gait and is LESS costly than lizard locomotion of similar mass; lateral undulation in Coluber constrictor costs ~3-5× more per unit distance; the efficiency advantage of sidewinding arises because the body-ground contact points are stationary (no sliding friction) — body waves travel backward while contact points push forward; CoT is still well above mammal allometric for same mass (~0.7 kcal/kg/km for 110g mammal) because ectotherm running muscles are less efficient and the sprawling posture generates high lateral forces
source: Secor et al. 1992 J Exp Biol 163:1-14

name: Greater spear-nosed bat
notes: 4 bats in circular flight room, mask respirometry; bat F mean VO2 = 27.53 ml O2/g/hr (highest individual), bat R = 24.68 ml O2/g/hr (slowest); mean ~26 ml O2/g/hr → 130 kcal/kg/hr; flight speed estimated 6 m/s = 21.6 km/h (circular room method, not wind tunnel; actual speed uncertain ±30%); CoT = 130/21.6 = 6.02 kcal/kg/km; Thomas (1975) showed bats fall on or slightly above the bird regression line in Tucker-style plots; bats are LESS efficient per km than birds of the same mass partly because mammalian membrane wings require active tension maintenance and the leading-edge membrane stores less elastic energy than feathers; CoT is much lower than running mammals of the same mass per unit DISTANCE because flight speed is so much higher; represents ~6× metabolic cost increase above resting for a similar-sized endotherm

name: Large fruit bat
notes: Mean mass 780g; circular flight room; lowest measured level-flight metabolic rate = 53.4 W/kg (Thomas 1975 search result) = 45.9 kcal/kg/hr at minimum power speed; for gross CoT at cruise speed 9 m/s = 32.4 km/h (Vmr estimate for 780g bat, wing span ~120cm); metabolic rate at cruise speed is typically higher than minimum power speed → using 214 kcal/kg/hr (Thomas search result for range of measurements); CoT = 214/32.4 = 6.60 kcal/kg/km; NOTE: if minimum metabolic rate (53.4 W/kg = 45.9 kcal/kg/hr) is used at minimum power speed (~7 m/s = 25 km/h), CoT = 45.9/25 = 1.84 kcal/kg/km (more consistent with bird allometric); large fruit bats show better mass-specific CoT than smaller bats — the general M^(-0.27) allometric scaling applies; flight metabolic rate falls on same double-log line as birds of equivalent mass confirming convergent aerodynamic solutions

name: Bumblebee
notes: Worker bumblebee mass 175 mg; VO2 measured in free forward flight across 0-4 m/s in wind tunnel (Nature 347:472); key finding: VO2 is approximately CONSTANT across all tested speeds (~65-75 ml O2/g/hr) — the expected U-shaped power curve was not observed; this is unique to insects and means CoT improves continuously with speed (fast flight costs same energy per hour but covers more distance); using 75 ml O2/g/hr at maximum sustained cruise ~8 m/s = 28.8 km/h: 375 kcal/kg/hr → CoT = 13.0 kcal/kg/km; minimum CoT would be at maximum speed; VO2 is high per unit mass because (1) wingbeat frequency ~200 Hz requires fast/expensive muscle, (2) insect flight muscle has ~95% efficiency in power generation but ~5% in conversion to flight power, (3) no elastic energy storage in wings; places bumblebee above the bird flight line at equivalent mass due to inefficient wingbeat mechanics

name: Tobacco hawkmoth
notes: Adult female ~2g; free tethered and free-flight VO2 measured in respirometry mask; during hovering (the dominant foraging flight mode): 38.4 ml O2/g/hr; during forward flight estimated 35-40 ml O2/g/hr at ~4 m/s = 14.4 km/h; using 38 ml O2/g/hr (gross including thermoregulation overhead at 40°C thorax): 190 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 190/14.4 = 13.2 kcal/kg/km; hawkmoths must maintain thorax at ~40°C for efficient muscle function (endothermic in flight, ectothermic at rest) — this adds to the metabolic cost; interesting comparison to bumblebee: similar mass and similar CoT; sphinx moths and bees converge on similar flight costs despite completely different evolutionary histories; hawkmoth migration has been documented — individuals can travel hundreds of km; note this is at hovering/foraging speed; at faster migration speeds CoT would be lower

name: Moon jellyfish
notes: Aurelia aurita bell diameter 3-6 cm, wet mass ~5g; swimming speed ~5 mm/s = 0.018-0.020 km/h from kinematic tracking; metabolic data from Uye & Shimauchi 2005; Gemmell et al. (2013 PNAS 110:17904) found jellyfish net CoT is 3.5× lower than sockeye salmon and lower than any other metazoan on a WET MASS basis; gross metabolic rate ~0.0032 kcal/kg WW/hr (active rate = 2× basal, basal = 0.0016 kcal/kg/hr); gross CoT = 0.0032/0.020 = 0.160 kcal/kg/km (WET MASS); mechanism: PASSIVE ENERGY RECAPTURE — during bell refill phase, vortex ring formed during contraction provides extra thrust for free, improving CoT by 48%; jellyfish muscle is ≤1% of wet mass (vs 50%+ for fish), so the denominator in CoT (wet mass) is dominated by metabolically inert mesoglea; if normalized to muscle mass, jellyfish would be LESS efficient than fish; the wet-mass comparison is biologically meaningful because it determines whether jellyfish can outcompete fish ecologically; fish only achieve lower CoT than Aurelia above ~100 kg body mass
source: Gemmell et al. 2013 PNAS 110:17904-17909; Uye & Shimauchi 2005 J Plankton Res 27:237

name: Andean condor (thermal soaring)
reference: Vöhringer et al. 2022 / Duriez et al. / Bishop et al. 2023
notes: Adult mass ~10.5 kg; thermal soaring specialist; from accelerometer + GPS tag study of Andean condors (published 2022): metabolic rate during soaring = ~2× resting metabolic rate; one condor flew 172 km in >5 hours without a single wingbeat; flaps only 1% of flight time (primarily at takeoff); resting BMR for 10.5 kg non-passerine = 78.3×10.5^0.723 = 426 kcal/day = 1.69 kcal/kg/hr; soaring metabolic rate = 2×1.69 = 3.38 kcal/kg/hr; at typical between-thermal travel speed 50 km/h (GPS-tracked groundspeed): CoT = 3.38/50 = 0.068 kcal/kg/km; THERMAL SOARING harvests gravitational potential energy from rising air (thermals); condor gains altitude for free then glides with glide ratio ~30:1 to cover horizontal distance; flapping flight would cost ~30× resting = 50.7 kcal/kg/hr → CoT = 1.01 kcal/kg/km (15× worse!); condor CoT beats the albatross and matches electric car; the energetic constraint for condors is finding takeoff thermals, not sustaining flight
source: Vöhringer et al. 2022 study / Bishop et al. 2023 PNAS 120:e2215686120 (Andean condor accelerometer study)

name: White stork (thermal soaring)
reference: Flack et al. 2018 / Liechti & Bruderer 1998 allometric estimate
notes: Mass 3.5 kg; migratory soaring bird crossing Sahara and sub-Saharan Africa; uses thermals during migration rather than flapping; metabolic rate ≈ 2× BMR during soaring (same physics as condor); BMR for 3.5 kg non-passerine = 78.3×3.5^0.723 = 171.6 kcal/day = 2.04 kcal/kg/hr; soaring metabolic rate = 2×2.04 = 4.08 kcal/kg/hr; migration travel speed ~45 km/h (GPS tracking, Flack et al. 2018 Science); CoT = 4.08/45 = 0.091 kcal/kg/km; storks soar during the day when thermals develop and must stop flying at night (unlike flapping migrants); European stork population crosses Bosphorus in thousands during autumn migration — these straits are preferred because thermals form over the narrower land crossing; if forced to flap (at sea): metabolic rate ~12× BMR = 24.5 kcal/kg/hr → CoT = 0.54 kcal/kg/km (6× worse) — this is why storks avoid overwater crossings
source: Flack et al. 2018 Science 360:1114; Liechti & Bruderer 1998

name: Petrol car (family sedan)
reference: Toyota Corolla class / ~2025 WLTP fuel economy data
notes: Toyota Corolla (2025 model year—fair description of a current compact family sedan); kerb mass 1,350 kg + 75 kg driver = 1,425 kg total; highway fuel economy 6.5 L/100km at 100 km/h (WLTP combined ~5.5 L, highway better; using realistic highway figure); petrol LHV = 8,830 kcal/L; energy per km = 6.5/100×8,830 = 573.9 kcal/km; power = 573.9×100/1425 = 40.3 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 40.3/100 = 0.403 kcal/kg/km; petrol car sits above the VLCC tanker (0.005) and freight train (0.007) because: (1) internal combustion engine ~30% thermal efficiency (vs diesel marine engines 50%+), (2) low mass means little amortisation of aerodynamic drag overhead, (3) rolling resistance much higher than steel-on-steel (Crr ~0.01 vs 0.0015); compare to electric car (0.077 kcal/kg/km) — petrol car is 5.3× less efficient per kg-km; a fully loaded car (4 people) would have CoT = 0.403×1425/1575 = 0.365
source: Toyota Corolla WLTP data (~2025 model year); petrol LHV from IPCC energy statistics

name: Electric car (BEV)
reference: Tesla Model 3 Standard Range / EPA data
notes: Tesla Model 3 Standard Range AWD; kerb mass 1,611 kg + 75 kg driver = 1,686 kg total; EPA-rated highway range 547 km on 75 kWh usable battery = 13.7 kWh/100km; at 100 km/h real-world: ~15 kWh/100km (slightly higher than EPA combined due to aerodynamic drag increase with speed); 1 kWh = 860 kcal (thermodynamic equivalent of electrical energy); energy per km = 15/100×860 = 129 kcal/km; power per kg = 129×100/1686 = 7.654 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 7.654/100 = 0.0765 kcal/kg/km; electric car is 5.3× more efficient than petrol car due to: (1) electric motor ~95% efficient vs ICE ~30%, (2) regenerative braking recovers ~25% of kinetic energy at stops, (3) no idle fuel consumption; REMARKABLY: electric car CoT is almost identical to the wandering albatross (0.079) and below the bicycle rider (0.15 kcal/kg/km) — the most efficient forms of human transport converge near 0.05-0.10 kcal/kg/km; comparison excludes battery production embodied energy
source: Tesla Model 3 EPA range data 2023; kWh to kcal: 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ / 4.186 kJ/kcal × 1000 = 860 kcal

name: Motorcycle
notes: Honda CB500F (representative mid-size naked motorcycle); kerb mass 190 kg + 75 kg rider = 265 kg total; highway fuel economy at 100 km/h: 4.5 L/100km (WMTC test cycle); petrol LHV = 8,830 kcal/L; energy per km = 4.5/100×8,830 = 397.4 kcal/km; power per kg = 397.4×100/265 = 149.8 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 149.8/100 = 1.50 kcal/kg/km; COUNTERINTUITIVELY motorcycle is 3.7× less efficient per kg than the car (0.40 kcal/kg/km) despite using less fuel per km (4.5 vs 6.5 L) — this is because the car's large mass (1,425 kg) is shielded by its streamlined body, dramatically improving mass-specific efficiency; motorcycle's small frontal area saves fuel per km, but per kg of vehicle mass the car wins; per PERSON transported the motorcycle is better: 1.50×265/75 = 5.3 kcal/kg_person/km vs car 0.40×1425/75 = 7.6 kcal/kg_person/km; the Tucker plot uses total system mass as the denominator
source: Honda CB500F specifications 2023; WMTC fuel economy

name: Concorde
notes: BAC/Aérospatiale Concorde; mid-cruise mass ~137,000 kg (MTOW 187,700 kg, landing ~108,000 kg); Mach 2 cruise at 18,000 m = 2,179 km/h; fuel burn at Mach 2 cruise: ~17,000 kg/hr of Jet-A (four Olympus 593 engines at 38,050 N thrust each); Jet-A LHV = 11,900 kcal/kg; energy rate = 17,000×11,900 = 202.3 million kcal/hr; per kg: 202,300,000/137,000 = 1,476 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 1,476/2,179 = 0.677 kcal/kg/km; 3.3× less efficient than Boeing 787-9 despite being 3.6× faster; SUPERSONIC DRAG PENALTY: at Mach 2, wave drag and aerodynamic heating add ~60-70% to total drag vs subsonic cruise; the Olympus 593 was one of the most thermodynamically efficient turbojets ever built but could not overcome wave drag physics; Concorde was viable commercially only with premium fares 4-5× economy class; retired 2003 after 27 years; per-passenger CoT improves to 0.677×137,000/100 passengers = 927 kcal/passenger-km
source: Concorde Flight Manual; ICAO technical data; Flight International Concorde fuel data

name: Helicopter
reference: Bell 206B JetRanger specification / FAA performance data
notes: Bell 206B JetRanger III; MTOW 1,451 kg; cruise speed 193 km/h (105 knots); fuel burn at cruise: ~90 kg/hr Jet-A (manufacturer data); Jet-A LHV = 11,900 kcal/kg; energy rate = 90×11,900 = 1,071,000 kcal/hr; per kg = 1,071,000/1,451 = 738 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 738/193 = 3.82 kcal/kg/km; helicopter is fundamentally inefficient compared to fixed-wing because ALL lift comes from powered rotor thrust — no wing to generate lift 'for free' from forward speed; disk loading is high (rotor area small relative to weight lifted), requiring more energy per newton of lift than a large gliding wing; REMARKABLE COMPARISON: helicopter CoT is almost identical to a DJI quadcopter drone (3.47 kcal/kg/km) despite being 5,800× heavier — both are rotorcraft with similar induced power scaling; compare to Boeing 787 (0.26 kcal/kg/km): helicopter needs 15× more energy per kg-km; autorotation (unpowered descent) is the closest a helicopter comes to fixed-wing gliding efficiency; the energy advantage of fixed-wing over rotorcraft is one of the fundamental results of aerodynamic theory
source: Bell 206B JetRanger III Pilot's Information Manual; FAA approved helicopter flight manual

name: Naked mole-rat (walking)
reference: Merchant et al. 2025 J Exp Biol
notes: Mass ~32g (H. glaber colony at University of Pretoria); treadmill respirometry at 10 cm/s = 0.36 km/h (submaximal, stable gait); net CoT = 2.9 ± 0.6 ml O2/kg/m (= 14.5 kcal/kg/km net); gross metabolic rate during walking = 3.03× resting (locomotory rate increased 203.2% above resting); resting VO2 inferred from net CoT and exercise ratio = 0.514 ml O2/g/hr = 2.57 kcal/kg/hr; gross metabolic rate = 3.03×2.57 = 7.79 kcal/kg/hr; gross CoT = 7.79/0.36 = 21.6 kcal/kg/km; naked mole-rat walking is ~3× more expensive per km than predicted by the mammal allometric (predicted net ~5 kcal/kg/km); the handicap arises because limbs are short and positioned directly below the body to support tunnel walls, not optimised for efficient locomotion; they also have unusually low BMR (~40% of predicted) helping total energy budget but not per-km locomotion cost; IMPORTANT CAVEAT: this is tunnel WALKING, not digging; burrowing costs 3-5× their resting rate (much higher); naked mole-rats share tunnels across generations to avoid repeated expensive excavation; the Damaraland mole-rat (Fukomys damarensis) has lower net CoT of 2.4 ml O2/kg/m = 12.0 kcal/kg/km (91.4% above resting), suggesting less trade-off between digging morphology and walking efficiency in that species
source: Merchant et al. 2025 J Exp Biol (PMC12268170); species comparison of Bathyergidae locomotion

name: Cape mole-rat (burrowing)
notes: Mass ~175g; burrowing through loose sandy soil in respirometry setup; resting VO2 = 1.13 ± 0.05 ml O2/g/hr; burrowing VO2 in loose sand = 3.41 ± 0.19 ml O2/g/hr (3.0× resting); VO2 shows a linear relationship to burrowing rate (Jarvis & Bennett 1990 Oecologia 81:444); burrowing speed estimated 5 mm/s = 0.018 km/h in loose sandy soil (based on typical mole-rat burrowing rates); gross CoT = 3.41×5/0.018 = 947 kcal/kg/km; net CoT = (3.41-1.13)×5/0.018 = 633 kcal/kg/km; IMPORTANT: this is LOOSE SANDY SOIL — the easiest substrate possible; in compacted clay or stony soil, costs would be 3-10× higher; burrowing is the most expensive terrestrial locomotion mode by far because soil must be physically displaced (loosened, carried, and compacted elsewhere); the energetic penalty of soil resistance vastly exceeds the cost of moving the animal's own body; this is why mole-rats maintain tunnel systems across generations rather than re-excavating — a profound illustration of how infrastructure investment pays energetic dividends

name: Pocket gopher (burrowing)
notes: Mass ~120g; Vleck (1979 Physiol Zool 52:391) measured O2 consumption during burrowing in four soil types from loose to compacted; resting VO2 = 0.84 ml O2/g/hr at 28-30°C; KEY RESULT: depending on soil type, burrowing requires 360-3400 times as much energy as moving the same distance across the surface; this CSV entry uses ~1000× surface net CoT as representative of moderate/typical soil (intermediate of Vleck's range); surface net CoT for 120g mammal (Tucker allometric) = 7.79×0.12^(-0.32)/4186×1000 ≈ 3.77 kcal/kg/km; burrowing net CoT = 1000×3.77 = 3770 kcal/kg/km; at estimated burrowing speed 10 mm/s = 0.036 km/h: net metabolic rate = 135.7 kcal/kg/hr; resting contribution = 4.2/0.036 = 117 kcal/kg/km; gross CoT = 3887 kcal/kg/km; the extreme cost arises from: (1) shear resistance of soil matrix ~40-200 kPa requiring large forces, (2) gopher uses incisors to cut soil then hindlimbs to sweep material back, (3) ~25% muscle mechanical efficiency means 4× more metabolic energy than work done; NOTE: Vleck's comparison normalises for distance not time — a gopher expends ~0.00007 kcal/km running vs ~0.46 kcal/km burrowing in same body; this is why fossorial species universally have reduced BMR (energy conservation adaptation)

name: Banana slug (crawling)
notes: Mass ~40g; crawling speed ~1 cm/s = 0.036 km/h on flat surface; Denny (1980 J Exp Biol 86:29) measured O2 consumption during mucus-trail locomotion; movement ~10× as costly as a mouse of equivalent weight, primarily due to mucus production; mouse equivalent mass (~40g) net CoT ≈ 4 kcal/kg/km → slug net CoT ≈ 40 kcal/kg/km; resting VO2 (ectotherm at 15°C): ~0.03 ml O2/g/hr = 0.15 kcal/kg/hr; gross CoT = 40 + 0.15/0.036 = 44.2 kcal/kg/km; gross metabolic rate = 44.2×0.036 = 1.59 kcal/kg/hr; MUCUS MECHANISM: the pedal mucus layer is 96-97% water; it acts as a viscous liquid under moving foot waves (lubricating adhesive locomotion) and as a solid gel under stationary areas (providing anchorage for push-off); mucus production accounts for more than 20× the mechanical work of actual locomotion, making it the dominant energetic cost; slugs moving along fresh conspecific mucus trails save ~73% of locomotion costs — a remarkable example of 'infrastructure sharing' in invertebrates; earthworm (Lumbricus terrestris) crawling has been extensively studied kinematically but published metabolic CoT data are absent from the literature
source: Denny 1980 J Exp Biol 86:29-45; Denny & Gosline 1980 J Exp Biol 88:375

name: C. elegans (swimming)
reference: Choi et al. 2020 Nat Commun; Vanfleteren 1980
notes: Adult day-2 worm; mass ~1 µg = 10^-9 kg; speed 1 mm/s = 0.0036 km/h (undulatory swimming in liquid; crawling on agar is ~0.3 mm/s = 3× slower giving CoT ~72000 kcal/kg/km); Active Metabolic Rate (AMR) during swimming from sub-nanowatt resolution direct calorimetry (Choi et al. 2020 Nature Comms 11:2826): ~100 nW per worm; per kg: 100 nW / 10^-9 kg = 100 W/kg = 86 kcal/kg/hr (includes both aerobic and anaerobic contributions — C. elegans is partly fermentative); gross CoT = 86/0.0036 = 23,889 kcal/kg/km; consistent with allometric VO2 = 1.66W^0.70 nl O2/µg/hr (Vanfleteren 1980 J Comp Physiol 144:351) giving aerobic component ~8 kcal/kg/hr (factor ~10 lower than total heat because C. elegans runs fermentative + aerobic mixed metabolism); REMARKABLE PHYSICS: the extreme CoT arises from two compounding effects: (1) allometric scaling means mass-specific metabolic rates increase with M^(-0.25) so a 1 µg worm has ~10,000× higher rate per kg than a 1 kg mammal, (2) the worm moves extremely slowly; this is a 7-order-of-magnitude extrapolation beyond Tucker's original dataset (fruit fly at 2 µg was the smallest); the AMR/SMR ratio is ~1.5 (locomotion adds ~50% above resting) — swimming is energetically expensive per km but cheap per hour; locomotion in C. elegans has been enormously studied (it has exactly 302 neurons, all mapped) but metabolic CoT was not computed until calorimetry improved enough to measure individual worms
source: Choi et al. 2020 Nature Comms 11:2826 (single-worm calorimetry); Vanfleteren 1980 J Comp Physiol 144:351

name: E. coli (swimming)
notes: Single E. coli cell; mass ~1.1 × 10^-15 kg (volume 1.0 µm^3, density 1.1 g/cm^3, Schavemaker & Lynch 2022 eLife Table 1); swimming speed ~30 µm/s = 0.000108 km/h (Berg & Brown 1972, Berg 2003 'E. coli in Motion'); total metabolic power from eLife table: total cell ATP expenditure = 1.59 × 10^10 ATP per cell cycle at 1 hr doubling time = 4.42 × 10^6 ATP/s; at 0.5 aJ per ATP = 2.21 × 10^-12 W per cell; per kg: 2,009 W/kg = 1,728 kcal/kg/hr; gross CoT = 1,728/0.000108 = 16,000,000 kcal/kg/km; FLAGELLAR OPERATING COST = 5.2% of total cell budget (Schavemaker & Lynch 2022 Table 1) = 90 kcal/kg/hr; net CoT (locomotion-specific) = 90/0.000108 = 833,000 kcal/kg/km; PURCELL'S INSIGHT (Life at Low Reynolds Number, 1977): at the bacterial scale Re ~10^-5, viscosity dominates — the bacterium stops in ~10^-7 seconds when its flagellum stops; there is no coasting; every metre requires continuous muscular (flagellar motor) work against viscous drag; the mechanical work against drag is only ~0.01 W/kg yet this costs ~100 W/kg metabolically because (1) the motor runs continuously, (2) flagellar synthesis (construction) is amortized over cell cycle, (3) the entire gross metabolic rate is dominated by biosynthesis (DNA, proteins, membranes) not locomotion; bacteria have evolved to exploit CHEMOTAXIS (swim-tumble-swim) to navigate gradients rather than travel long distances — the extreme gross CoT makes long-distance locomotion energetically meaningless; NOTE: gross CoT here is per WET CELL MASS (97% water in the cytoplasm) — this is the only consistent way to compare with other organisms in Tucker's framework but should be interpreted with care
source: Schavemaker & Lynch 2022 eLife 11:e77266 (flagellar costs); Berg & Brown 1972; Purcell 1977 Am J Phys 45:3

name: Paramecium caudatum
reference: Katsu-Kimura et al. 2009 J Exp Biol 212:1819-24
notes: P. caudatum; cell mass M=1.51e-10 kg measured directly (prolate spheroid ra=83 µm, rb=20 µm, density 1.04 g/cm³); swimming speed Us=1 mm/s=0.0036 km/h (standard solution 1mM K+); total metabolic power at Us measured by simultaneous O2 consumption (optic fluorescence sensor) and swimming speed: 4.02e-6 J/hr per cell; metabolic rate per kg = 4.02e-6/(4184 × 1.51e-10) = 6.36 kcal/kg/hr; gross CoT = 6.36/0.0036 = 1768 kcal/kg/km; CRUCIALLY: 70.7% of total metabolic energy goes to ciliary propulsion (SMR=1.18e-6 J/hr = 29.3% of total); this makes Paramecium a LEGITIMATE CoT measurement unlike E. coli where locomotion is <5% of total — cilia cost is the dominant energy expenditure; mechanical efficiency = 0.078% (Stokes power 2.2e-9 J/hr vs total 4.02e-6 J/hr); COT decreases monotonically with speed (hyperbolic, no optimum within normal speed range) unlike fish which have a J-shaped COT curve with a minimum; this is the SMALLEST organism with a directly measured, well-validated CoT in the literature (6.6× smaller by mass than C. elegans); Katsu-Kimura et al. were the first to simultaneously measure O2 consumption and swimming speed in a single-celled organism
source: Katsu-Kimura et al. 2009 J Exp Biol 212:1819-24 (PMID 19482999)

name: Tardigrade
reference: Schiemann et al. 2024 PLOS One; Pedersen et al. 2020 J Exp Biol
notes: Hypsibius exemplaris (water bear); mass estimated from body dimensions: mean length 251.5 µm (Schiemann et al. 2024 PLOS One, n=103), width ~30% of length = 75 µm, cylindrical volume = π × 37.5² × 251.5 µm³ = 1.11 × 10⁶ µm³, density 1.05 g/cm³, mass ~1.18 µg = 1.18 × 10⁻⁹ kg; walking speed: 0.23 ± 0.08 body lengths/s on agarose-coated Petri dish = 57.9 µm/s = 2.08 × 10⁻⁴ km/hr (Schiemann et al. 2024, also Nirody et al. 2021 PNAS 118:e2107289118 found 160.5 µm/s on stiffer 50-kPa gel — substrate-dependent); tardigrades are smallest known walking animal with 8 legs; metabolic rate (resting): 10.8 ± 1.8 nmol O₂/mg/hr for Richtersius coronifer at 22°C from Pedersen et al. 2020 J Exp Biol 223:jeb225466 (first quantitative O2 respirometry of individual tardigrades) = 242 nL O₂/mg/hr = 1.21 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 1.21/0.000208 = 5817 kcal/kg/km — NOTE: this is a LOWER BOUND as the resting metabolic rate is used (no measurement of active walking O2 in tardigrades exists); active walking likely 3-5× resting, so active CoT ~17,000-29,000 kcal/kg/km, consistent with allometric expectation for a ~1 µg ectotherm; DUBIOUS FLAGS: (1) metabolic rate is from R. coronifer, locomotion is from H. exemplaris — different families but both eutardigrades; (2) resting not active metabolism used
source: Schiemann et al. 2024 PLOS One 19(11):e0310738; Nirody et al. 2021 PNAS 118:e2107289118; Pedersen et al. 2020 J Exp Biol 223:jeb225466

name: Red kangaroo (slow hop)
reference: Dawson & Taylor 1973 Nature 246:313; Kram & Dawson 1998 Comp Biochem Physiol B 120:41
notes: Red kangaroo (Macropus rufus); female, mass 20.4 kg measured (Kram & Dawson 1998); VO2 remains nearly CONSTANT at ~10.2 mL O2/kg/min regardless of hopping speed from 2-6 m/s (Dawson & Taylor 1973 Nature 246:313-314) — this is the defining feature: metabolic rate ~3.06 kcal/kg/hr at ALL speeds; CoT therefore decreases monotonically with speed (faster = cheaper per km, unique among all animals); mechanism: elastic energy storage and recovery in compliant Achilles tendons (~33-41% of energy recovered per hop; Ker et al. 1986 wallaby estimate); multiple speed replicates added to illustrate constant VO2 and decreasing CoT; Dawson & Taylor 1973: "rate of O2 consumption is nearly independent of running speed" 2-6 m/s, average 10.2 mL O2/kg/min; max VO2 = 3 mL O2/kg/s during uphill hopping (Kram & Dawson 1998); muscle efficiency normal ~30%; red kangaroos prefer slow speeds (~3-4 m/s) to avoid dangerous tendon stress despite slower speeds being less economical per km

name: Red kangaroo (medium hop)
reference: Dawson & Taylor 1973 Nature 246:313; Kram & Dawson 1998 Comp Biochem Physiol B 120:41
notes: Red kangaroo (Macropus rufus); female, mass 20.4 kg measured (Kram & Dawson 1998); VO2 remains nearly CONSTANT at ~10.2 mL O2/kg/min regardless of hopping speed from 2-6 m/s (Dawson & Taylor 1973 Nature 246:313-314) — this is the defining feature: metabolic rate ~3.06 kcal/kg/hr at ALL speeds; CoT therefore decreases monotonically with speed (faster = cheaper per km, unique among all animals); mechanism: elastic energy storage and recovery in compliant Achilles tendons (~33-41% of energy recovered per hop; Ker et al. 1986 wallaby estimate); multiple speed replicates added to illustrate constant VO2 and decreasing CoT; Dawson & Taylor 1973: "rate of O2 consumption is nearly independent of running speed" 2-6 m/s, average 10.2 mL O2/kg/min; max VO2 = 3 mL O2/kg/s during uphill hopping (Kram & Dawson 1998); muscle efficiency normal ~30%; red kangaroos prefer slow speeds (~3-4 m/s) to avoid dangerous tendon stress despite slower speeds being less economical per km

name: Red kangaroo (fast hop)
reference: Dawson & Taylor 1973 Nature 246:313; Kram & Dawson 1998 Comp Biochem Physiol B 120:41
notes: Red kangaroo (Macropus rufus); female, mass 20.4 kg measured (Kram & Dawson 1998); VO2 remains nearly CONSTANT at ~10.2 mL O2/kg/min regardless of hopping speed from 2-6 m/s (Dawson & Taylor 1973 Nature 246:313-314) — this is the defining feature: metabolic rate ~3.06 kcal/kg/hr at ALL speeds; CoT therefore decreases monotonically with speed (faster = cheaper per km, unique among all animals); mechanism: elastic energy storage and recovery in compliant Achilles tendons (~33-41% of energy recovered per hop; Ker et al. 1986 wallaby estimate); multiple speed replicates added to illustrate constant VO2 and decreasing CoT; Dawson & Taylor 1973: "rate of O2 consumption is nearly independent of running speed" 2-6 m/s, average 10.2 mL O2/kg/min; max VO2 = 3 mL O2/kg/s during uphill hopping (Kram & Dawson 1998); muscle efficiency normal ~30%; red kangaroos prefer slow speeds (~3-4 m/s) to avoid dangerous tendon stress despite slower speeds being less economical per km

name: Chimpanzee (knuckle-walking)
notes: Chimpanzee (Pan troglodytes); knuckle-walking (quadrupedal); mean mass 59.8 kg, 5 adult chimps age 6-33 yr, treadmill at 1.0 m/s (Sockol et al. 2007 PNAS 104:12265-12269); NET cost of transport = 0.19 ± 0.014 mL O2/kg/m (quadrupedal) — net = subtracts standing metabolic rate; net CoT 0.95 kcal/kg/km; gross CoT estimated ~1.36 kcal/kg/km; chimpanzee CoT is ~4× higher than humans walking (0.19 vs ~0.08 mL O2/kg/m), well ABOVE the expected allometric line for quadrupeds of similar mass; key result of Sockol 2007: human walking is 75% less costly than both bipedal and quadrupedal walking in chimps; variation in cost explained by biomechanics: chimps have crouched knuckle-walking posture with flexed hips requiring large muscle volumes per unit ground force; humans have more extended hindlimb and longer contact time, reducing cost; this supports the hypothesis that energy savings drove evolution of hominin bipedalism; CATEGORY NOTE: chimpanzee bipedal walking is 10% more expensive than knuckle-walking on average (0.21 vs 0.19 mL O2/kg/m); NOTE: value is NET CoT — gross would be ~1.36 kcal/kg/km

name: Human (running)
reference: Taylor et al. 1982 J Exp Biol 97:1; Margaria et al. 1963
notes: Human running; 70 kg adult; 2.92 m/s = 10.5 km/h (comfortable recreational pace); VO2 ~38 mL O2/kg/min (gross) = 12 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 12/10.5 = 1.14 kcal/kg/km; classic result that running CoT is nearly CONSTANT with speed (unlike walking which has a U-shaped CoT curve) — Margaria et al. 1963, Taylor et al. 1982 J Exp Biol 97:1-21; human running CoT (~1.0-1.2 kcal/kg/km) is ~50% higher than walking CoT (~0.73 kcal/kg/km); contrast with Tucker bicycle data: running CoT is ~4-8× higher than bicycle CoT (0.13-0.25 kcal/kg/km); net running cost ~0.88 kcal/kg/km (Margaria 1963: net 4.0 J/kg/m); allometric prediction for 70 kg mammal: ~0.4 kcal/kg/km — human running is 2-3× above this, showing bipedal running is not especially efficient compared to quadrupeds; see Sockol 2007 for comparison with chimpanzee
source: Taylor et al. 1982 J Exp Biol 97:1-21

name: Ostrich
reference: Fedak & Seeherman 1979 Nature 282:713; Taylor et al. 1982 J Exp Biol 97:1
notes: Ostrich (Struthio camelus); adult female ~97 kg; running at 3 m/s = 10.8 km/h; VO2 ~12 mL O2/kg/min = 3.5 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 0.325 kcal/kg/km; Fedak & Seeherman (1979 Nature 282:713-716) famously showed that ostriches and ponies of the same body mass use the same metabolic energy to run — bipedality vs quadrupedality makes NO difference to CoT at the same mass; the allometric equation for running (COT = 8.46 × M^-0.40 J/kg/m, Taylor et al. 1982) applies equally to bipeds and quadrupeds; ostrich CoT fits the mammalian quadruped line perfectly; faster running: ostriches can reach 70 km/h (max sprint) but field travel speeds are 3-5 m/s; they do not gallop — their fastest gait is aerial running; the Rubenson et al. (2004 Proc Biol Sci 271:1091-1099) paper showed that ostriches select gaits (walking → grounded running → aerial running) to minimise metabolic cost, similar to horses; net CoT ≈ 0.21 J/kg/m (calculated from Taylor et al. slope equation for a bird of this mass)

name: Spider monkey (brachiation)
notes: Spider monkey (Ateles sp.) brachiation; mass ~7.5 kg; brachiation speed ~1 m/s = 3.6 km/h; Parsons & Taylor (1977 Physiol Zool 50:182-188) measured O2 consumption of spider monkeys brachiating on rope mill and horizontal ladder mill vs walking on treadmill; KEY RESULT: brachiation is SIGNIFICANTLY MORE EXPENSIVE than walking at any given speed (~20-30% higher VO2) — debunks the naive pendulum efficiency argument; lorises moving inverted under a rope used the SAME energy as normal walking; the suspended pendulum (brachiation) is NOT cheaper than the inverted pendulum (walking); VALUES ARE ESTIMATED: walking CoT for 7.5 kg animal ≈ 0.90 kcal/kg/km (allometric eq); brachiation ≈ 30% higher = 1.17 kcal/kg/km; actual values from paper not directly available in accessible text; DUBIOUS because: (1) spider monkeys are not true brachiators (gibbons are — but gibbons have never had direct metabolic CoT measured during brachiation); (2) CoT values are estimated from allometric equation + 30% overhead, not directly from the Parsons & Taylor paper; ECOLOGICAL CAVEAT: despite higher energy per m, brachiation may reduce DISTANCE traveled in trees by allowing access to peripheral branches — so total daily energy budget may not be higher

name: Human (crawling)
reference: Sparrow & Irizarry-Lopez 1987 Hum Mov Sci 6:307; Choi et al. 2024 Int J Sports Med
notes: Adult human quadrupedal crawling (hands and knees); 70 kg; speed ~1.5 km/h (0.42 m/s, typical laboratory crawling pace); VO2 ~20 mL O2/kg/min gross; met rate ~6 kcal/kg/hr; CoT ~4.0 kcal/kg/km; Choi et al. 2024 (Int J Sports Med): 10 min quadrupedal walking at same speed as bipedal produced 254.48% increase in energy expenditure (increase of 4.15 kcal/min absolute); Sparrow & Irizarry-Lopez (1987 Hum Mov Sci 6:307-326) is the classic reference for adult crawling VO2; key result: crawling is ~4-5× more expensive per km than upright walking; mechanism: arms are not efficient weight-bearers (hip extensors carry large loads, arm muscles operate in mechanically disadvantaged posture); supports evolutionary hypothesis that upright bipedalism evolved largely to reduce locomotor energy costs; contrast with chimpanzee knuckle-walking: even chimpanzees use their hands/knuckles for weight-bearing locomotion and it costs them 4× more than human walking; speed is estimated as typical laboratory crawling speed (studies vary 0.4-1.0 m/s); exact CoT is speed-dependent

name: Human (Groucho running)
reference: McMahon et al. 1987 J Appl Physiol 62:2326
notes: Human Groucho running (running with persistently flexed knees, named after Groucho Marx); 70 kg; same speed as normal running (10.5 km/h); VO2 increased by up to 50% compared to normal running at same speed; met rate ~17.1 kcal/kg/hr vs 11.4 kcal/kg/hr for normal running; CoT ~1.63 kcal/kg/km vs ~1.09 for normal running; McMahon TA, Valiant G, Frederick EC (1987 J Appl Physiol 62:2326-2337); mechanism: flexed-knee running reduces effective vertical stiffness of the body (spring-mass system), eliminating the normal elastic energy storage and return in tendons and muscles; also reduces transmission of mechanical shock from foot to skull by ~27-39%; the high metabolic cost demonstrates that the normal running spring mechanism (stiff leg, rapid foot contact, elastic storage in Achilles/plantar fascia) is essential for metabolic efficiency; ECOLOGICAL NOTE: Groucho running is used by primates and early hominins in crouched bipedal posture; the high cost supports the evolutionary pressure toward fully upright running; at same speed as normal running (10.5 km/h), Groucho running uses ~50% more energy per km
source: McMahon et al. 1987 J Appl Physiol 62:2326-2337

name: Human (skipping)
reference: Minetti 1998 Proc Biol Sci 265:1227; McDonnell et al. 2019 Gait Posture 70:414
notes: Human skipping; 70 kg; 9.0 km/h (2.5 m/s); VO2 ~43.9 mL O2/kg/min (approx 20-22% higher than running at same speed); met rate ~13.2 kcal/kg/hr; CoT ~1.46 kcal/kg/km; Minetti AE (1998 Proc Biol Sci 265:1227-1235); also McDonnell J et al. (2019 Gait Posture 70:414-419) "Skipping has lower knee joint contact forces and higher metabolic cost compared to running"; Pequera G et al. (2022 Eur J Appl Physiol): "Metabolic power in skipping and running increased with similar slope but different intercepts. This determined the different behavior of CoT: constant in running, DECREASING in skipping"; KEY RESULTS: (1) skipping is metabolically more expensive than running at most speeds, (2) skipping CoT decreases as speed increases (unlike running where CoT is nearly constant), so at very fast speeds they approach each other; (3) skipping uses BOTH inverted-pendulum (walking) and spring-mass (running) mechanisms simultaneously - it is a hybrid gait; (4) this hybrid mechanism links bipedal skipping to quadrupedal galloping biomechanically; ECOLOGICAL NOTE: humans do not normally use skipping for locomotion; it may be preferred gait in low-gravity environments (astronauts on Moon spontaneously skipped); children skipping at ~4-5 yrs old may do so for developmental/play reasons; the lower knee joint contact forces (McDonnell 2019) make it potentially useful for rehabilitation; value is estimated from relative comparison to running VO2

name: Human (snowshoeing)
reference: Connolly et al. 2002 J Strength Cond Res 16:606
notes: Snowshoeing on flat packed snow; 70 kg adult; 2.95 mph = 4.75 km/h; VO2 = 18.18 ± 0.8 mL O2/kg/min (directly measured); met rate = 5.45 kcal/kg/hr; CoT = 1.149 kcal/kg/km; Connolly DAJ et al. (2002 J Strength Cond Res 16:606-610) "The energy expenditure of snowshoeing in packed vs. unpacked snow at low-level walking speeds"; also Pandolf KB et al. (1977) and subsequent military terrain-coefficient work; KEY COMPARISON: at same speed (4.75 km/h), normal treadmill walking VO2 ~11-12 mL/kg/min, snowshoeing VO2 = 18.18 = ~1.6× more expensive; the paper notes "equivalent to treadmill walking at 6 mph (9.7 km/h)" - i.e., snowshoeing at 4.75 km/h feels like fast walking at 9.7 km/h; unpacked snow is even more expensive; energy cost varies significantly with: snow depth, sinkage, snowshoe design (rotating vs fixed toe cord), slope; modern aluminum snowshoes are lighter than traditional wooden ones, somewhat reducing the penalty; compare to XC skiing which is 2-3× faster at similar metabolic rate, making skiing substantially more efficient per km than snowshoeing
source: Connolly et al. 2002 J Strength Cond Res 16:606-610

name: Ghost crab
reference: Weinstein & Full 1992 Physiol Zool 65:1199; Full & Herreid 1983 Am J Physiol 244:R160
notes: Ghost crab (Ocypode quadrata); mean mass ~10.5 g; running at 0.5 m/s = 1.8 km/h; minimum net cost of transport (Cmin) ~10 J/kg/m at 24°C from Weinstein & Full (1992 Physiol Zool 65:1199-1210); net met from locomotion = 10 × 0.5 = 5 W/kg = 4.30 kcal/kg/hr; standing rate for 10.5g ectotherm at 25°C ~1.5 kcal/kg/hr (from allometric for small crustaceans); gross met = 5.80 kcal/kg/hr; gross CoT = 3.22 kcal/kg/km; Full & Herreid (1983 Am J Physiol 244:R160-R166) measured maximum aerobic VO2 = 0.67 mL O2/g/hr during running; ghost crabs are remarkably aerobically capable for arthropods; Weinstein & Full (1992) showed Cmin decreases ~2/3 when Tb rises 24→30°C — on tropical beaches (natural Tb ~30°C) CoT is ~1.1 kcal/kg/km; CONTEXT: fastest land crabs; pure aerobic locomotion at moderate speeds (minimal anaerobic at ~0.5 m/s); representative crustacean entry; compare to cockroach (arthropod walker) at ~3-4 kcal/kg/km
source: Weinstein & Full 1992 Physiol Zool 65:1199-1210; Full & Herreid 1983 Am J Physiol 244:R160-R166

name: Domestic cat
reference: Taylor et al. 1982 J Exp Biol 97:1
notes: Domestic cat (Felis catus); av. body mass 3.90 kg (2 individuals directly measured; Taylor et al. 1982 J Exp Biol 97:1-21); speed 5.4 km/h = 1.5 m/s (comfortable trot; Goslow et al. 1973 J Exp Biol 57:505 document trot onset at ~1.62 m/s for domestic cats); gross VO2 at 1.5 m/s calculated from Taylor 1982 allometric: Y-intercept = 0.287 × 3.90^(-0.303) = 0.190 mL O2/s/kg (11.4 mL/min/kg); slope = 0.534 × 3.90^(-0.316) = 0.347 mL O2/m/kg (net CoT = 1.67 kcal/kg/km); gross VO2 = 0.190 + 0.347×1.5 = 0.711 mL/s/kg = 42.7 mL/min/kg = 12.8 kcal/kg/hr; gross CoT = 2.37 kcal/kg/km; places domestic cat on general mammalian allometric line for terrestrial locomotion; endotherm with BMR ~2.4 kcal/kg/hr (Kleiber); as a small carnivore the cat has slightly elevated basal metabolism vs herbivores of similar mass; maximum sprint ~50 km/h; CONTEXT: cats are ambush predators with excellent burst capacity but moderate sustained-trot efficiency
source: Taylor et al. 1982 J Exp Biol 97:1-21
